title: Dual Dramatic Question — Parallel A/B Arcs
author: (implicit in many ensemble films; theorized variously)
source: The dual-dramatic-question structure: two stacked goals resolved in reverse order. Closest theoretical antecedent in Snyder's A-story/B-story logic, but where Snyder treats B as a relationship-and-theme subplot, dual dramatic question treats both A and B as substantive external goals.

lineage.note: Two stacked dramatic questions (a global Problem A and a local Problem B; Solution B resolves before Solution A). Two parallel arcs that mutually inform each other; resolution of one provides the conditions or insight for resolving the other.

stages.name: Problem A — Global
stages.function: The larger, systemic question. Often the one the audience can't immediately act on.

stages.name: Problem B — Local
stages.function: The smaller, character-scale question. The one that gets the protagonist into the story.

stages.name: Pursuit of B
stages.function: Protagonist pursues B; encounters A through that pursuit.

stages.name: Solution B
stages.function: B is resolved — but in resolving B, A becomes both clearer and harder.

stages.name: Pursuit of A
stages.function: With B resolved, protagonist now pursues A — armed with what B taught.

stages.name: Solution A
stages.function: A is resolved, often differently than the protagonist expected. The nested structure makes both resolutions feel earned.

snyder_save_the_cat._note: Dual-DQ A-story ≈ Snyder A-story; Dual-DQ B-story ≈ Snyder B-story, but with B treated as a substantive external goal, not just a theme/relationship subplot.

applicability.limitations: Requires two genuinely substantive questions. If B is just a relationship subplot, use Snyder directly. If A and B aren't actually distinct, the structure collapses.
